planting.note: Plant shallot sets 4–8 weeks before last frost in spring, or in fall in zones 7–10 for a June harvest; Ambition is a widely grown European F1 shallot producing uniform, oval, red-violet to copper-skinned bulbs in clusters of 5–10 per set with the classic shallot flavor — mild, sweet, and slightly garlicky — in a reliable, high-yielding variety preferred in professional kitchens throughout Europe.

directions: Plant shallot sets 1 inch deep, 4–6 inches apart, with the tip just at soil level; each set multiplies into a cluster of 5–10 bulbs; keep consistently moist during establishment and in spring; reduce watering as leaves begin to yellow and fall over; harvest when tops fall and bulb skins are dry and papery, then cure for 2–3 weeks before storage. Ambition is a European F1 hybrid shallot produced by commercial seed companies primarily in the Netherlands, where the commercial shallot industry has been refined over centuries into highly productive, uniform varieties for both professional kitchen use and home garden production. The Ambition variety produces medium-sized, uniformly shaped, oval to pear-shaped bulbs with red-violet to copper-mahogany skin and purplish-pink inner flesh; each planted set multiplies into a cluster of 5–10 distinct bulbs, the characteristic growth habit that distinguishes shallots from onions (which produce a single bulb from a set). The flavor is the classic shallot character — more subtle, sweet, and complex than onions, with a slightly garlicky undertone that distinguishes shallots as the preferred allium in French haute cuisine for vinaigrettes, pan sauces, and dishes where onion flavor would be too harsh. Storage life is excellent — 6–9 months under proper conditions (cool, dry, dark). The F1 hybrid nature of Ambition means it does not come true from saved seed, but sets (bulbs) from the previous year's crop can be replanted successfully.

common_name: Golden Gourmet Shallot (Dutch Yellow Shallot, Banana Shallot)

planting.note: Plant shallot sets 4–8 weeks before last frost in spring, or in fall in zones 7–10 for overwintering harvest; Golden Gourmet produces uniform, elongated oval bulbs with golden-amber to bronze outer skin and pale yellow to white flesh — a Dutch-type yellow shallot with classic mild, sweet shallot flavor and excellent productivity and storage life that make it a top-selling shallot variety in European commercial production.

directions: Plant shallot sets 1 inch deep, 4–6 inches apart, with the tip just at or slightly above soil level; each set multiplies into a cluster of 5–8 bulbs; maintain consistent moisture during spring growth; reduce watering when tops begin to yellow and fall over; harvest when bulb skins are dry and golden, then cure 2–3 weeks in a warm, dry location before storage. Golden Gourmet is a Dutch-type yellow shallot produced by seed companies primarily in the Netherlands, the global center of commercial shallot breeding and production; the variety represents the classic 'Dutch shallot' style — elongated-oval (sometimes banana-shaped), golden to copper-amber skinned bulbs with very pale yellow to white inner flesh, as opposed to the red-violet type represented by Ambition and Zebrune. The flavor profile is mild, sweet, and delicately onion-like with the characteristic shallot complexity — somewhat different from red-type shallots, with a cleaner, lighter character that particularly suits cream sauces, light vinaigrettes, and dishes where the golden color and mild flavor complement pale or delicate ingredients. Each planted set multiplies into a cluster of 5–8 uniform bulbs that cure to a beautiful, uniform golden-amber appearance — the classic appearance of the shallots found in upscale grocery stores and restaurant supply. Stores 8–10 months under good conditions, outlasting most red-type shallots. Can be replanted from the previous year's sets.

planting.note: Start indoors 10–12 weeks before last frost; Lancelot is an early-season leek variety (70–85 days from transplant) with more slender, tender stalks than overwintering types — it is harvested in summer and early fall while still tender, rather than being held through winter; ideal for gardeners who want leeks available early in the season before winter storage types are ready.

directions: Start seeds indoors 10–12 weeks before last frost at 65–75°F; transplant outdoors after last frost, setting transplants 4–6 inches apart in a trench 4–6 inches deep to blanch the shafts; gradually backfill the trench as plants grow; harvest beginning at 1 inch shaft diameter for best tenderness. Lancelot is a European-bred early-season leek variety that fills an important niche in the leek production calendar: unlike the classic overwintering leek varieties (such as Bleu de Solaise or Giant Musselburgh) that require 130–150+ days from transplant and are intended to overwinter in the garden for winter and spring harvest, Lancelot matures in just 70–85 days from transplant and is harvested in late summer and early fall while stalks are still slender, tender, and at their best eating quality. The stalks are typically 1–1.5 inches in diameter at harvest — more refined in texture and milder in flavor than fully mature overwintering leeks — making Lancelot the superior choice for preparations where tenderness and delicate leek flavor are paramount: vichyssoise, leek gratin, braised leeks, and fresh leek salads where the young stalks are excellent raw. The blue-green foliage and cylindrical white shaft create an attractive plant in the garden from late spring through summer. Lancelot does not have the cold hardiness or overwintering capability of late types but excels as a summer-season leek in climates too hot for fall-planted overwintering types.

common_name: Candy Onion (AAS Winner Intermediate-Day Sweet Onion)

planting.note: Start indoors 10–12 weeks before last frost; Candy is an All-America Selections award-winning intermediate-day (12–13 hour) hybrid onion that fills the gap between long-day northern onions and short-day southern onions — it performs across a wider geographic range than most onion types and produces large, round, flat-topped bulbs with white skin and exceptionally sweet, mild flesh that can be eaten like an apple.

directions: Start seeds indoors 10–12 weeks before last frost; transplant outdoors 4–6 weeks before last frost (onion transplants tolerate light frost); set 4–6 inches apart with the bulb base at soil level; keep consistently moist through bulb formation; reduce watering as tops begin to yellow and fall over; harvest and cure 2–3 weeks before storage. Candy is an All-America Selections (AAS) award-winning hybrid onion developed specifically for its exceptional sweetness and adaptability as an intermediate-day variety capable of producing high-quality bulbs across a wider latitudinal range than either long-day or short-day types. Intermediate-day onions (requiring 12–13 hours of daylight to trigger bulbing) are particularly valuable in zones 5–8 — the mid-latitude transition zone where long-day varieties (14+ hours required) often form small bulbs in the shorter days of the mid-latitudes and short-day varieties (10–12 hours) often bolt prematurely in the longer summer days further north. The result is that Candy succeeds in climates from Maine to Georgia where the 'wrong type' onion is a common gardening mistake. The large, round to slightly flattened, white-skinned bulbs have extraordinarily sweet, mild flesh with low pyruvate content — the same characteristic that makes Vidalia and other famous sweet onions nearly non-pungent; Candy onions can be eaten raw like a mild apple, used in salads without overwhelming other flavors, or caramelized into an intensely sweet onion jam. Yields are excellent for a sweet onion type.

planting.note: Start indoors 10–12 weeks before last frost; Patterson is a long-day (14–16 hour) hybrid yellow storage onion widely recognized as one of the most reliable and highest-yielding yellow onions for northern gardens — it produces large, globe-shaped, golden-yellow-skinned bulbs that store exceptionally well for 8–12 months in ideal conditions, making it the premier variety for northern gardeners who want a full year's supply from a single planting.

directions: Start seeds indoors 10–12 weeks before last frost; transplant outdoors 4–6 weeks before last frost; space 4–6 inches apart in rows 12–18 inches apart; Patterson is a long-day variety requiring 14+ hours of daylight to form bulbs — performs in zones 3–8 north of the 35th parallel; harvest when 80% of tops fall naturally and cure 3–4 weeks in a warm, dry location with good airflow. Patterson is a long-day hybrid yellow storage onion widely considered one of the gold standards for reliable, high-yielding yellow onion production in northern gardens (zones 3–8); it has been a staple recommendation of Johnny's Selected Seeds — the primary commercial source — and university extension services throughout the Northeast and Midwest for its combination of high yield, consistent bulb quality, and exceptional storage performance. The large, globe-shaped bulbs have smooth, dark golden-amber skins that cure tightly and resist breakdown in storage; the flesh is white, firm, moderately pungent, and full-flavored in the traditional yellow onion style — excellent for all cooking purposes: caramelizing, soups, stews, roasting, and pickling. The standout characteristic that makes Patterson the choice of serious northern vegetable gardeners is storage life: under ideal conditions (33–40°F, low humidity, good airflow), Patterson onions store 8–12 months — essentially the full year from one harvest to the next — with far less deterioration, sprouting, or softening than most other onion varieties. This durability in storage is the result of tight, multiple layers of dry outer scales that seal the bulb against moisture and pathogens.
